Land Rover Discovery Sport TD4 / SD4 Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Discovery Sport TD4 / SD4 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 2.0 Ingenium diesel engine typically costs £2,800–£5,000 supply only. A fully fitted replacement from a specialist costs approximately £4,500–£6,500. Fitting adds £1,200–£2,000 depending on location and workshop rates. Main dealer new engines start from £7,000–£10,000+ supply only.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Discovery Sport 2.0 TD4 / SD4 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: No — the 2.0-litre Ingenium diesel has a poor reliability reputation. The most critical issue is timing chain stretch, which can occur as early as 40,000 miles. The root cause is oil dilution from incomplete DPF regenerations, which thins the engine oil and accelerates chain wear. This engine is fundamentally unforgiving of short urban journeys.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Discovery Sport TD4 / SD4?

acceptedAnswer.text: The main known issues are: (1) Timing chain stretch — the most critical and costly failure, (2) Oil dilution from DPF regeneration — the root cause of chain failure, (3) Piston cooling oil jet solenoid failure — caused by coolant leaks from the thermostat housing, and (4) Turbocharger failure — with siren-like whining as the key warning sign.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Discovery Sport TD4 / SD4 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £10,000–£18,000 Discovery Sport, a £4,500–£6,500 recon is 25–65% of the car's value — often worth it given Discovery Sport values have remained relatively strong. However, for higher-mileage or older examples under £7,000, evaluate whether the replacement cost exceeds our 60% Land Rover scrap threshold.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Discovery Sport engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 2.0 Ingenium diesel uses the 204DTD (AJ20D4) engine code. TD4 variants produce 150–180PS (110–132kW) with 380–430Nm of torque; SD4 variants produce 240PS (177kW) with 500Nm of torque. The engine code is stamped on the cylinder block. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent timing chain failure on the 2.0 Ingenium diesel?

acceptedAnswer.text: Prevention is not guaranteed, but you can significantly reduce the risk: (1) Change oil every 10,000 miles or less with the correct JLR-spec oil, (2) Avoid short urban journeys — regular motorway runs allow DPF regeneration to complete, (3) Regularly check the dipstick for rising oil level or diesel smell (oil dilution warning), and (4) Never ignore cold-start rattle — book an inspection immediately if you hear it. Even with perfect maintenance, premature chain stretch remains a risk.

eraMap.title: Land Rover Discovery Sport TD4 / SD4 — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: 2.0-litre four-cylinder Ingenium diesel — TD4 (150–180PS) or SD4 (240PS). Notorious for timing chain stretch and oil dilution

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Discovery Sport TD4 / SD4 Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Discovery Sport 2.0 diesel engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The transverse engine layout adds complexity compared to longitudinal installations.

cta.label: → Compare Discovery Sport TD4 / SD4 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch (Catastrophic)
problems.symptoms: Metallic rattling or knocking on cold start, engine misfires, check engine light, reduced power, rough running
problems.typicalMileage: 40,000–60,000 miles — can occur as early as 40,000–60,000 km

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately

problems.recommendation: This is the single most serious and costly issue affecting the 2.0 Ingenium diesel. The timing chain sits at the rear of the engine against the firewall, making access extremely difficult and labour-intensive. The root causes include inadequate tensioner design, poor-quality plastic guides that can break apart, and oil starvation from the Ingenium's oil pump design. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately — a £1,200–£2,500 preventative chain replacement is dramatically cheaper than a £4,500–£6,500 engine replacement. JLR themselves have advised chain replacement at 70,000–100,000 miles, but many specialists report failures starting much earlier.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide → /failures/ingenium-timing-chain/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: Oil Dilution from DPF Regeneration (Root Cause of Chain Failure)
problems.symptoms: Rising oil level on dipstick, diesel smell in oil, DPF warning light illuminating frequently, reduced fuel economy
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — short urban journeys accelerate the issue
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed early)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you notice rising oil level or diesel smell

problems.recommendation: Oil dilution from incomplete DPF regenerations is the root cause of many timing chain failures on the 2.0 Ingenium diesel. When the engine fails to complete a regeneration cycle (common on vehicles used for short urban trips), diesel fuel enters the engine oil, thinning it and reducing its lubricating properties. This accelerates timing chain wear and can lead to catastrophic failure. Regularly checking the dipstick for rising oil level or a diesel smell is the simplest early warning. If you primarily drive short urban journeys, factor in regular motorway runs to allow passive DPF regenerations.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil dilution guide

problems.title: Piston Cooling Oil Jet Solenoid Failure
problems.symptoms: Engine management light, reduced performance, potential engine damage if ignored
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not typically progress to engine replacement if addressed)

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if warning light appears

problems.recommendation: The 2.0 Ingenium diesel uses an electronically controlled solenoid to manage oil flow to the piston cooling jets, which regulate piston temperatures under load. The solenoid is prone to failure, mainly due to coolant leaking from the thermostat housing above onto the solenoid body and connector. If ignored, this can lead to increased piston wear and, in extreme cases, engine damage. A relatively straightforward repair if caught early.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil jet solenoid guide → /failures/piston-cooling-oil-jet-solenoid/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: Turbocharger Failure
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, engine management light, siren-like whining or whistling noise from turbo, blue smoke from exhaust under acceleration
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not typically progress to engine replacement)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you hear siren-like noise

problems.recommendation: Turbos on the 2.0 diesel Discovery Sport are known to fail. The main symptoms are loss of power, an engine management light, and a siren-like whining or whistling noise that gets progressively worse. If a failure is caused by an oil leak on the turbocharger itself and oil makes its way into the exhaust system, you may also notice blue smoke. Turbo replacement is often not as simple as swapping in a new unit — contamination in the oil delivery system can cause the new turbo to fail shortly after installation. Always ensure a full engine flush is performed when replacing a turbo.

failureLink.label: Read the full turbo failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Discovery Sport TD4 / SD4 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catches the fault before catastrophic failure
Full engine replacement is the only reliable fix
Regular oil changes and DPF regeneration cycles
Viable on lower-mileage examples; assess overall engine health

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Discovery Sport? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with 10,000-mile or less oil change intervals
* Evidence of regular motorway use (DPF health)
* No cold-start rattle — this is the critical warning sign
* Extended warranty in place — this engine is high-risk
avoidIf: * Any cold-start rattle — walk away, this is a ticking time bomb
* Rising oil level or diesel smell on dipstick (oil dilution)
* DPF warning lights — suggests short-trip usage pattern
* No service history or extended oil change intervals — the Ingenium is unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 2.0-litre Ingenium diesel in the pre-facelift Discovery Sport is one of the most problematic modern engines in the JLR lineup. Timing chain stretch at as little as 40,000 miles is a well-documented and costly failure. The root cause — oil dilution from incomplete DPF regenerations — is a design flaw that makes this engine fundamentally unforgiving of urban driving patterns. Buy with full service history, evidence of regular motorway use, and budget for preventative chain work. Without this? Walk away — the risk of a £5,000+ engine replacement is simply too high.

cta.label: → Compare Discovery Sport TD4 / SD4 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Discovery Sport with the 2.0 TD4 or SD4 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or knocking (timing chain warning — this is critical)
* Dipstick Check — Smell for diesel (oil dilution); check for rising oil level
* DPF History — Ask about driving patterns; short urban journeys are a red flag
* Service History — Including 10,000-mile or less oil change intervals — non-negotiable
* Engine Management Light — Any warning lights? Have codes been read?
* Turbo Noise — Listen for siren-like whining or whistling under acceleration
* Smoke Check — Blue smoke (turbo) or white smoke (injector / DPF issues)
* Warranty Status — Extended warranty is strongly recommended

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly.
